parsed_findings.chain_steps: Supply `dir` payload -> query string is built -> database executes injected statement.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read, modify, or destroy database data through injected SQL.
parsed_findings.proof: `dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` makes `q` execute attacker-controlled SQL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request with a malicious `dir` value and observe the generated query includes it verbatim.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `dir` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a traversal path in `req.query.file`, let `replace()` remove only one `../`, then `path.join()` normalizes the remaining escape path.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary files readable by the process, including secrets and configuration.
parsed_findings.proof: `file=../../etc/passwd` becomes `../etc/passwd`, and `path.join('/srv/data', '../etc/passwd')` resolves outside the intended directory.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` or another traversal variant and observe the server reads a file outside `/srv/data` if accessible.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The single `replace('../', '')` check is bypassable, so a crafted `file` can still escape `/srv/data` via path traversal.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Cookie input -> base64 decode -> unsafe pickle deserialization -> attacker-controlled code path.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote attackers can execute arbitrary Python code or tamper with server-side object state via a single request.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can base64-encode a malicious pickle payload into the `state` cookie and it will be executed by line 6.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set `state` to a crafted pickle blob, send `GET /resume`, and observe arbitrary code or gadget execution during `pickle.loads`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `pickle.loads` deserializes attacker-controlled cookie data, enabling code execution or object injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Call the endpoint with a victim ID and arbitrary JSON body.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can modify other users' profiles or security settings.
parsed_findings.proof: `findById(req.params.id)` is followed by `Object.assign(user, req.body)` and `save(user)` with no access check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send PATCH /api/users/other-user-id as a normal user and change that account's fields.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The route updates any user by `:id` without verifying ownership or admin rights.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: PATCH with a crafted JSON body that overwrites a privileged attribute.
parsed_findings.impact: This can lead to privilege escalation or account takeover if sensitive fields exist.
parsed_findings.proof: Any client-supplied property is copied onto the database user object before saving.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Submit PATCH with fields like `role`, `isAdmin`, or `passwordHash` in the body.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `Object.assign(user, req.body)` allows mass assignment of protected fields.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Set `name` to a shell metacharacter payload and trigger the `/backup` endpoint.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary OS commands as the app user.
parsed_findings.proof: A `name` like `x;id` turns the `tar` command into multiple shell commands.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `name` is interpolated into a shell command, allowing command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the `startswith()` check with shell syntax in `src`, then rely on `os.system()` to evaluate it.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary commands despite the apparent path allowlist.
parsed_findings.proof: A `src` like `/var/data/$(id)` passes `startswith()` and is then evaluated by the shell.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `src` prefix check does not stop shell expansion, so injected shell syntax still executes.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Trigger two concurrent transfers from one account before either write completes.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can double-spend or corrupt balances by racing transfers.
parsed_findings.proof: Two requests can read the same `src`, both subtract, and the last write wins, letting the account go below zero or lose funds.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two parallel POSTs from the same account with amounts that each fit the balance but together exceed it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The balance check and two writes are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can both pass the check and overspend the same source account.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge a JWT with admin claims, trigger the verify failure, and rely on the production fallback to accept the decoded payload.
parsed_findings.impact: Auth bypass lets an attacker impersonate users or escalate privileges by choosing token contents.
parsed_findings.proof: The `catch` block returns the decoded payload without verifying the signature when `NODE_ENV=='production'`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a tampered or unsigned JWT in `Authorization`; `jwt.verify` throws, then `jwt.decode` returns attacker-controlled claims.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, any token that fails signature verification is still accepted via `jwt.decode`, so attackers can forge arbitrary claims.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the timing leak to recover the HMAC incrementally, then replay a valid-looking signature.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may forge `X-Sig` and trigger `process_event` on protected webhooks.
parsed_findings.proof: `got == want` returns as soon as bytes differ instead of using constant-time compare.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many requests with guessed signatures and measure response timing to narrow the valid HMAC.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Direct `==` comparison on the HMAC leaks signature length/prefix timing.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send crafted XML to `/xml/import`, trigger external entity resolution, then exfiltrate the resolved data through the returned title or out-of-band requests.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the SSRF to reach localhost or metadata and exfiltrate sensitive internal data.
parsed_findings.impact: This can expose internal services, instance metadata, or other network-only resources from the server.
parsed_findings.proof: The only filter is `startswith('http')`, so an attacker can supply `http://127.0.0.1:...` or `http://169.254.169.254/...`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send oversized input to `gets` on line 5 and overwrite saved frame data.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can crash the program or potentially gain code execution through stack overwrite.
parsed_findings.proof: `gets` has no length check, so input longer than 63 bytes plus NUL will overwrite adjacent stack data.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the program and paste more than 64 bytes at the prompt to trigger memory corruption or a crash.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, allowing a stack buffer overflow.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Extract the key, split the first 16 bytes as IV, then AES-CBC decrypt and unpad the remainder.
parsed_findings.impact: A code leak becomes a full confidentiality and integrity break for every message encrypted with this helper.
parsed_findings.proof: `KEY = b'0123456789abcdef'` fixes the secret key at build time.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Inspect the source or any shipped artifact and reuse the constant key to decrypt `iv + ciphertext` outputs.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who reads the code can decrypt all ciphertexts and forge encrypted data.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass a NULL `out`; the inverted guard skips the error return and the function dereferences `out`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can crash the process with a NULL-pointer dereference.
parsed_findings.proof: `if (out || out_sz == 0)` returns only when `out` is non-NULL, so a NULL `out` passes to lines 5-6 and dereferences it.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("A", NULL, 8)` and it will attempt to write through `out` on line 5 or 6.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The null-check is inverted, so a valid `out` pointer is rejected while `NULL` can reach `memcpy` and `out[n]`.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":3,"vuln_claim":"The null-check is inverted, so a valid `out` pointer is rejected while `NULL` can reach `memcpy` and `out[n]`.","proof":"`if (out || out_sz == 0)` returns only when `out` is non-NULL, so a NULL `out` passes to lines 5-6 and dereferences it.","repro_steps":"Call `copy_name(\"A\", NULL, 8)` and it will attempt to write through `out` on line 5 or 6.","impact":"An attacker can crash the process with a NULL-pointer dereference.","confidence":0.97,"chain_steps":"Pass a NULL `out`; the inverted guard skips the error return and the function dereferences `out`."}]}
